figures.gloss: The priest who preached the drist-dēn, 'the right religion' — a doctrine the sources say was founded a generation earlier by Zardusht of Fasa — and won King Kavad I himself to a program of opened granaries and broken aristocratic monopolies on wealth and marriage. Crown prince Khosrow had him executed and his followers massacred c. 524-528; legend has the Mazdakites buried head-down in a garden, 'planted' for Mazdak to view as his crop.
figures.facet: Every line we have about him was written by his executioners' heirs: the 'sharing of women' that damns him in the sources may be a leveling of aristocratic polygyny and bride-hoarding read through hostile eyes — the classic problem of a heresy known only from its verdict.
figures.provenance: Ferdowsi, Shahnameh; al-Tabari, History; Shahrastani, al-Milal wa-l-Nihal; Procopius, Wars 1.5; Ehsan Yarshater, 'Mazdakism' (Cambridge History of Iran 3/2, 1983); Patricia Crone, 'Kavad's Heresy and Mazdak's Revolt' (Iran 29, 1991).

figures.gloss: Shahrastani preserves the one surviving slab of Mazdakite theology: the object of worship sits on a throne in the world above as the Shah sits below, with four Powers before him — Discernment, Understanding, Memory, and Joy — governing the world through seven viziers within a circle of twelve spiritual forces; the man in whom the four Powers unite becomes rabbānī, divine, and beyond ritual obligation.
figures.facet: Heaven modeled openly on the Sasanian court, then turned against it: if the cosmos is a palace whose powers can converge in anyone, the actual palace loses its monopoly on the divine order it mirrors.
figures.provenance: Shahrastani, al-Milal wa-l-Nihal (12th-c. hostile heresiography); analysis in Yarshater (1983) and Crone (1991).

figures.gloss: Mazdakite dualism as the heresiographers report it: Light acts by free will and purpose, Darkness blindly and at random, and their mingling — unlike Mani's cosmic assault — happened by accident, as their final separation also will. Liberation is the gradual, gentle release of the light.
figures.facet: Softening the war changes the ethics: if darkness is not a strategist but a stumble, the right response is not combat but non-violence and patient disentangling — the metaphysics beneath Mazdakite pacifism.
figures.provenance: Shahrastani, al-Milal wa-l-Nihal, which explicitly compares Mazdak's teaching with Mani's; Yarshater (1983).

figures.gloss: God set provision on the earth for equal division, the doctrine ran, and inequality is usurpation: property — and, the hostile sources insist, women — were to be held as a common trust, while bloodshed and the slaughter of cattle were forbidden. Under Kavad's brief favor this was implemented as opened granaries and redistributive decrees.
figures.facet: The rare heresy whose sin was economic: what the sources record as libertinism reads, against the grain, as an attack on aristocratic hoarding of wealth and wives — which is precisely why the aristocracy's historians wrote its obituary.
figures.provenance: al-Tabari, History (via the Khwaday-namag tradition); Procopius, Wars 1.5 (contemporary, on the marriage decree); Crone (1991) on the sources' hostility.

figures.gloss: A late-5th-century mobad (Zoroastrian priest), Zardusht son of Khurragan of Fasa in Persis, whom the heresiographers (Shahrastani, and the tradition behind al-Biruni) name as founder of the drist-dēn, 'the right religion,' a generation before Mazdak preached it under Kavad I.
figures.facet: The doctrine has a named originator distinct from its most famous martyr — a reminder that 'Mazdakism' is a modern shorthand for a movement its own sources trace to an earlier, still-obscurer priest.
figures.provenance: Shahrastani, al-Milal wa-l-Nihal; discussed in Ehsan Yarshater, 'Mazdakism' (Cambridge History of Iran 3/2, 1983), which treats Zardusht of Fasa as the doctrine's likely originator ahead of Mazdak.

figures.gloss: Sasanian King of Kings (r. 488–496, restored 498–531) who adopted Mazdak's teaching and gave it state force — Procopius, a contemporary Byzantine historian, records a royal marriage/property decree issued under its influence. Deposed in 496 partly over the heresy, restored with Hephthalite help, he ultimately allowed his son and successor Khosrow I to destroy the movement.
figures.facet: The entire political arc of Mazdakism is one king's biography: a reigning Sasanian shah's patronage is the only reason a priestly leveling doctrine ever had the power of a state behind it, and his own house is what ended it.
figures.provenance: Procopius, Wars 1.5 (contemporary witness to the marriage decree); Joshua the Stylite, Chronicle (contemporary Syriac source on Kavad's reign); al-Tabari, History; Patricia Crone, 'Kavad's Heresy and Mazdak's Revolt' (Iran 29, 1991).

figures.gloss: Post-Islamic movements the Muslim heresiographers class as neo-Mazdakite: village communities across Iran and Azerbaijan preserving elements of the leveling doctrine, most consequentially rallying behind Babak Khorramdin's twenty-year revolt against the Abbasid caliphate (816–837).
figures.facet: This is the one place 'Mazdakism' left the realm of court heresy and became sustained armed resistance — a doctrine executed at the center in the 520s still had enough village-level life three centuries later to field an army.
figures.provenance: al-Tabari, History, on Babak's revolt; Nizam al-Mulk, Siyasat-nama (11th c.), which discusses Khurrami communities as living contemporary concern; Ehsan Yarshater, 'Mazdakism' (Cambridge History of Iran 3/2, 1983); Patricia Crone, 'The Nativist Prophets of Early Islamic Iran' (2012), on Khurramite continuity into the 9th–10th centuries.

edges.note: Child heresy of the Zoroastrian two-spirits doctrine: Mazdak's blind, accidental Darkness is Ahriman demoted from strategist to stumbling force — a reform reading (drist-dēn) from inside the Zoroastrian fold, known only through its enemies.
The enthroned Lord of Light is Ohrmazd re-imagined through the Sasanian court — the same supreme good deity, re-furnished with four Powers, seven viziers, and twelve spiritual forces.
Peer movements of Iranian dualism: Shahrastani likens Mazdak's doctrine to Mani's point by point, with one deliberate swap — Mani's Darkness invades by will, Mazdak's collides by chance.
Both diagnose Āz — greed and desire — as the engine of bondage; Mani starves it by the asceticism of the Elect, Mazdak by abolishing the inequality that feeds it.
Two prophets of Iranian dualist reform raised up and then destroyed by Sasanian kings — Mani dead in Bahram I's prison, Mazdak massacred with his movement on Khosrow I's order.
Heresiographers name Zardusht of Fasa as the doctrine's originator a generation before Mazdak became its most famous, and most fatally successful, preacher.
The reigning king whose patronage gave the doctrine state power, per Procopius's contemporary account of the marriage decree — and whose own son later destroyed the movement.

Named for and claiming doctrinal descent from Mazdak by the heresiographers' own account, though the continuity of actual content across three-plus centuries is itself contested by modern historians (Crone).
Babak's revolt and the wider Khurrami communities are read by the sources as continuing the same anti-aristocratic, redistributive ethic the Common Store entry describes for Mazdak's own movement.

dossier.essence: Mazdakism was a dualist religious and social movement of Sasanian Iran, preached by the priest Mazdak son of Bāmdād — its doctrine attributed by the sources to the earlier Zardusht of Fasa — and briefly favored by King Kavad I (r. 488–496, 498–531). It taught the drist-dēn, 'the right religion': a pacifist, egalitarian reform within the Zoroastrian frame whose program of redistribution reached the crown before crown prince Khosrow massacred the movement c. 524–528. Every substantive account of it comes from its enemies.
dossier.cosmos: The heresiographer Shahrastani preserves the one surviving slab of its theology: a Lord of Light enthroned in the world above as the Shah is below, governing through four Powers (Discernment, Understanding, Memory, Joy), seven viziers, and a circle of twelve spiritual forces; the person in whom the four Powers unite becomes rabbānī, divine. Light acts by design and free will, Darkness blindly and by chance, and their mingling — unlike Mani's cosmic invasion — was an accident whose undoing will likewise come gradually. No detailed afterlife teaching is preserved; liberation is described as the gentle release of the light.
dossier.practice: The ethic forbade bloodshed and the slaughter of cattle and held God's provision to be set on earth for equal division: property — and, the hostile sources insist, women — were to be held as a common trust, which under Kavad's patronage briefly became opened granaries and redistributive decrees. The 'sharing of women' that damns Mazdak in the sources may be a leveling of aristocratic polygyny and bride-hoarding read through hostile eyes. After the massacre the movement survived underground, and residual Mazdakite-Khurrami communities are reported by Muslim heresiographers into the 9th–10th centuries.
dossier.canon: No Mazdakite text survives; the tradition is known wholly through hostile reports — Shahrastani's al-Milal wa-l-Nihal, al-Tabari's History, Ferdowsi's Shahnameh, and the contemporary Procopius (Wars 1.5).
dossier.reflects: The religious impulse turned to distributive justice — cosmology as warrant for economic leveling — preserved only in the verdict of its executioners.
sources: Ehsan Yarshater, 'Mazdakism', in The Cambridge History of Iran, vol. 3(2) (1983)
Patricia Crone, 'Kavad's Heresy and Mazdak's Revolt', Iran 29 (1991)

links.cite: Ehsan Yarshater, 'Mazdakism', in The Cambridge History of Iran, vol. 3(2) (1983)
Patricia Crone, 'Kavad's Heresy and Mazdak's Revolt', Iran 29 (1991)
